_catalog.name: CargoTrack Incoterms Intelligence Catalog
_catalog.disclaimer: Incoterms® 2020 rules are published by the International Chamber of Commerce. The rules should be agreed upon in the contract of sale and incorporated by reference to 'Incoterms® 2020'. These descriptions are for educational and risk-screening purposes.

sellerResponsibilities: Make goods available at named place
Provide commercial invoice and packing list
buyerResponsibilities: Export customs clearance (note: buyer assumes export in seller's country — unusual)
All freight costs from seller's door
terms.commonMisuse: EXW puts export customs obligation on buyer, which is impractical for foreign buyers who cannot control export procedures in the seller's country. Many banks and customs authorities prefer FOB or FCA for letters of credit.
terms.insuranceNotes: Buyer bears all insurance risk from seller's premises. Seller has no insurance obligation.
terms.customsResponsibility: BUYER handles both export and import customs. Highly unusual arrangement.

riskProfile.forSeller: LOW — seller bears almost no risk or cost after making goods available
riskProfile.forBuyer: HIGH — buyer bears all costs and risks including export complications in seller's country

complianceFlags: Export control: if buyer is foreign, seller may still have export control obligations even under EXW
AES/EEI filing: U.S. exporter must still file Electronic Export Information regardless of Incoterms
terms.lcNotes: Not recommended for letters of credit — buyer controls all transport; bank cannot easily verify shipment

terms.riskTransferPoint: When goods are delivered to named carrier at named place. If seller's premises: when loaded on buyer's transport. If other place: when delivered ready for unloading.
sellerResponsibilities: Deliver goods to carrier at named place
buyerResponsibilities: Contract with carrier from named place
All freight costs from named place
terms.commonMisuse: Sometimes confused with FOB. Key difference: FCA works for all transport modes including container. FOB is technically for bulk/break-bulk sea only.
terms.insuranceNotes: No insurance obligation on either party by default. Buyer should arrange insurance from named place. Incoterms 2020 added option for seller to obtain bill of lading at buyer's request.
terms.customsResponsibility: SELLER handles export customs. BUYER handles import customs.

riskProfile.forSeller: LOW — risk transfers at named place close to seller

complianceFlags: Seller must complete export customs formalities — cannot disclaim export control obligations
Recommended for containerized cargo (unlike FOB which is technically break-bulk)
terms.lcNotes: Compatible with LCs. Incoterms 2020 option: seller can instruct carrier to issue on-board B/L for LC purposes

terms.riskTransferPoint: When goods are placed alongside the vessel at the named port of shipment.
sellerResponsibilities: Deliver goods alongside vessel at named port
terms.commonMisuse: Only appropriate for bulk cargo, break-bulk, or bulk commodities where goods are loaded directly into holds. Not suitable for containerized cargo (use FCA instead).
terms.insuranceNotes: Seller not obligated to insure. Risk transfers alongside vessel — before loading.
terms.customsResponsibility: SELLER handles export. BUYER handles import.

riskProfile.forBuyer: HIGH — bears loading and all subsequent risks

complianceFlags: Sea/inland waterway only — do not use for air or multimodal
Appropriate for bulk (grain, coal) or break-bulk, not containers
terms.lcNotes: Difficult with LCs — risk transfers before loading, so B/L won't confirm risk transfer point

terms.riskTransferPoint: When goods are placed on board the vessel at the named port of shipment.
buyerResponsibilities: Ocean freight from port of shipment
requiredDocumentsForSeller: On-Board Bill of Lading (confirming goods on board)
terms.commonMisuse: Most commonly misused Incoterm. FOB is technically for bulk/break-bulk sea freight only. Using FOB for container shipments (most modern sea freight) is technically incorrect — risk should transfer at container yard (use FCA instead). However, market practice often still uses FOB for containers, especially in Asia trade.
terms.insuranceNotes: Seller not obligated to insure. Risk transfers on board vessel — buyer should have insurance from that point.
terms.customsResponsibility: SELLER handles export. BUYER handles import.
complianceFlags: Technically sea/waterway only — containers should use FCA
Risk transfer at ship's rail — seller bears loading risk
Must specify port of shipment precisely
Widely used in practice for containerized Asia-trade despite technical incorrectness
terms.lcNotes: Very compatible with LCs — on-board B/L clearly evidences delivery

terms.riskTransferPoint: Risk transfers when goods are on board vessel at port of shipment. Seller pays freight to destination port but does NOT bear risk during transit.
sellerResponsibilities: Pay ocean freight to named destination port
buyerResponsibilities: All risk from port of shipment (even though seller pays freight)
Insurance (critical — buyer bears risk during seller-paid transit)
terms.commonMisuse: Buyer often misunderstands: seller pays freight but risk is on BUYER during transit. Buyer must arrange insurance. Many buyers assume CFR = seller's risk during transit — INCORRECT.
terms.insuranceNotes: BUYER must arrange insurance. Seller has no insurance obligation. This mismatch (seller pays freight, buyer bears risk) makes insurance critical for buyer.
terms.customsResponsibility: SELLER handles export. BUYER handles import.

riskProfile.forSeller: LOW — risk transfers at loading, only cost commitment is freight
riskProfile.forBuyer: HIGH — bears transit risk despite not paying freight

complianceFlags: Sea/waterway only — use CPT for multimodal
CRITICAL: buyer bears risk even though seller pays freight — insurance mandatory for buyer
Must specify destination port precisely
terms.lcNotes: Compatible with LCs — on-board B/L evidences shipment

terms.riskTransferPoint: Risk transfers on board vessel at port of shipment. Seller pays freight AND minimum insurance to destination port.
sellerResponsibilities: Arrange and pay minimum cargo insurance (Institute Cargo Clauses C — minimum coverage)
Provide insurance certificate naming buyer as beneficiary
buyerResponsibilities: All risk from port of shipment
Consider topping up insurance (Clauses C is minimum — buyer may want Clauses A)
requiredDocumentsForSeller: Insurance Certificate / Policy (naming buyer, covering at least CIF value + 10%)
terms.commonMisuse: CIF is sea/waterway ONLY. Using CIF for air freight is technically incorrect (use CIP). Also: seller's insurance obligation is MINIMUM (Clauses C) — buyer should verify adequacy. Insurance certificate must name buyer as beneficiary.
terms.insuranceNotes: Seller must provide MINIMUM insurance (Institute Cargo Clauses C). Coverage must be at least 110% of CIF value. Insurance policy must be assignable and in buyer's currency. BUYER should consider upgrading to Clauses A (all-risk) at their own cost.
terms.customsResponsibility: SELLER handles export. BUYER handles import.

riskProfile.forBuyer: MEDIUM — has minimum insurance, but Clauses C coverage is limited

complianceFlags: Sea/waterway ONLY — use CIP for other modes
Insurance certificate must name buyer as beneficiary — verify
Clauses C is minimum coverage — check adequacy for high-value or fragile goods
Insurance amount must be at least 110% of CIF invoice value
Most common Incoterm in LC transactions
terms.lcNotes: Highly compatible with LCs. Documents required: Commercial Invoice, B/L, Insurance Certificate — classic documentary credit document set

terms.riskTransferPoint: Risk transfers when seller delivers goods to FIRST carrier at place of shipment. Seller pays freight to named destination. Risk/cost split point differs.
sellerResponsibilities: Pay all freight to named destination
buyerResponsibilities: All risk from first carrier delivery point
requiredDocumentsForSeller: Transport Document (not necessarily B/L — any mode)
terms.commonMisuse: Often confused with CIP (which adds insurance). Risk transfers at first carrier even though seller pays to destination. Buyer must arrange insurance.
terms.insuranceNotes: No insurance obligation on seller. Buyer must arrange insurance coverage from point of first carrier delivery.
terms.customsResponsibility: SELLER handles export. BUYER handles import.
complianceFlags: All modes — multimodal friendly (unlike CFR which is sea only)
Risk transfers at first carrier, not destination — buyer must insure
Seller must specify named place of destination clearly
terms.lcNotes: Compatible with LCs but transport document type varies by mode

terms.riskTransferPoint: Risk transfers at first carrier delivery. Seller pays freight AND insurance (ALL RISKS — Institute Cargo Clauses A) to named destination.
sellerResponsibilities: Pay all freight to named destination
Arrange and pay comprehensive ALL-RISK insurance (Clauses A — higher than CIF requirement)
buyerResponsibilities: All risk from first carrier delivery point
requiredDocumentsForSeller: Insurance Certificate / Policy (ALL RISKS — Clauses A, minimum 110% of CIP value)
terms.commonMisuse: CIP requires ALL RISKS (Clauses A) insurance — higher standard than CIF (which only requires Clauses C). Sellers sometimes incorrectly provide minimum Clauses C under CIP.
terms.insuranceNotes: Seller MUST provide Clauses A (all-risk) insurance under Incoterms 2020. This is an upgrade from CIF. Coverage must be at least 110% of CIP value. Policy must be assignable to buyer.
terms.customsResponsibility: SELLER handles export. BUYER handles import.

riskProfile.forSeller: MEDIUM — freight and all-risk insurance cost
riskProfile.forBuyer: MEDIUM — has all-risk insurance coverage but still bears some risk gaps

complianceFlags: ALL RISKS insurance (Clauses A) mandatory — verify seller has not cut costs with Clauses C
All modes — preferred over CIF for multimodal/air
Risk transfers at first carrier — not destination
terms.lcNotes: Compatible with LCs for all modes. Insurance certificate is key document

terms.riskTransferPoint: When goods are placed at buyer's disposal at named destination, ready for unloading, but before customs clearance.
sellerResponsibilities: All freight and insurance (optional) to named destination
Deliver to named place ready for unloading
terms.commonMisuse: Seller bears all transit risk but has no insurance obligation — must arrange voluntarily. Often confused with DDP (which includes import clearance). Under DAP, buyer still handles import customs.
terms.insuranceNotes: No insurance obligation on seller — but seller bears all transit risk. Seller should arrange insurance to protect their exposure.
terms.customsResponsibility: SELLER handles export. BUYER handles import.

riskProfile.forSeller: HIGH — bears all transit risk and costs
riskProfile.forBuyer: LOW — only handles import customs and unloading

complianceFlags: Seller bears full transit risk — should arrange insurance
Buyer handles import customs (unlike DDP)
Named destination must be specific — including unloading location if relevant
Popular for e-commerce and direct-to-consumer international shipments
terms.lcNotes: Not ideal for LCs — seller's obligations only evidenced upon delivery at destination, which banks cannot easily verify at time of presentation

terms.riskTransferPoint: When goods are unloaded at the named destination. Seller bears risk through unloading.
sellerResponsibilities: Bear all transit risk including during unloading
UNLOAD goods at named destination (this is the key difference from DAP)
terms.commonMisuse: DPU is the ONLY Incoterm where the seller is responsible for unloading at destination. This requires the seller to have the right to unload at the destination facility — not always practical. New in Incoterms 2020 (previously 'DAT — Delivered at Terminal').
terms.insuranceNotes: No insurance obligation but seller bears maximum risk through unloading. Seller must arrange insurance.
terms.customsResponsibility: SELLER handles export. BUYER handles import.

riskProfile.forSeller: VERY HIGH — maximum exposure including unloading

complianceFlags: ONLY Incoterm where seller unloads at destination — verify right to unload
Seller bears maximum risk — insurance essential
Replaced DAT in Incoterms 2020
terms.lcNotes: Very difficult for LCs — risk/delivery evidenced only at unloading

terms.riskTransferPoint: When goods are placed at buyer's disposal at named destination, cleared for import.
sellerResponsibilities: Import customs clearance IN DESTINATION COUNTRY
Bear all risk until delivery at destination
buyerResponsibilities: Nothing else — maximum convenience for buyer
requiredDocumentsForSeller: Import Customs Declaration (destination country — seller must be authorized importer)
terms.commonMisuse: DDP requires seller to act as importer of record in the destination country — which may be legally impossible without a local entity or importer registration. Seller may not be able to obtain import licenses. DDP also transfers VAT/GST obligation to seller in many countries.
terms.insuranceNotes: Seller bears all risk — must arrange insurance. No obligation stated but essential to protect maximum exposure.
terms.customsResponsibility: SELLER handles BOTH export AND import customs. Maximum obligation for seller.

riskProfile.forSeller: MAXIMUM — all risks, all costs, all customs

complianceFlags: CRITICAL: Seller must be registered importer in destination country — often not practical
Import VAT/GST obligation falls on seller — tax compliance complexity
Cannot use if seller cannot act as importer of record (no legal entity in destination)
Very popular for e-commerce (Delivered Duty Paid = 'no surprise charges for buyer')
If seller cannot handle import formalities, use DAP instead
terms.lcNotes: Not suitable for LCs — delivery only evidenced after full customs clearance at destination
